item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not in the least surprised to find that Pelican Bay was part of the inspiration for this piece. Solitary confinement is psychological torture, pure and simple.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Note: The last thing I did before reading this article was read SCP-5031, which is basically its exact opposite.

My god. He was a good guy. He could have been fine, an asset even. But they made him an object, treated him worse than an animal, and what they got was a monster. God damn them all to hell.

The most chilling part of this is the stellar use of clinical tone to cover up reasonable, unscientific reactions to the fucked-up shit taking place in the story. And it ends in the absolute fucking perfect spot. God damn. God damn. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There are very few times I cannot bring myself to complete reading an SCP, and this is one of them. Not because this work is bad or anything, but because it is so ruthless, so brutal yet so ambitious. God, I can't stand this horribleness. Great imagery, characterization and storytelling you have there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I get that a certain callousness and apathy can be expected from the Foundation, and I also understand that this may in some cases make things worse. But the incompetence in this article is not the kind of incompetence that I have seen before in other SCPs. This is not the kind of incompetence that makes me feel disturbed by the researchers' lack of empathy; this is the kind of incompetence that makes me facepalm or role my eyes. The Foundation in this article is acting in a way that is so - stupidly amoral - that it obviously endangers their mission to contain anomalies. You cannot deconstruct a trope, when there isn't a trope in the first place - and the kind of self-destructive behaviour that the Foundation shows in here is something I have never seen in other SCPs before.

Of course there is no canon, and maybe I shouldn't downvote an article only because of how it clashes with my personal headcanon of how the Foundation operates. But a certain level of professionality is expected from every version of the Foundation - that's why Keter Duty and lolFoundation aren't a thing anymore. The Foundation is cold, not cruel - and this SCP Foundation is cruel, not cold.

Also, I cannot believe this hasn't been brought up yet: The tests are way too evil. The first test already showed that 4504 can mold flesh, and that it hurts the subject. It also should have revealed to the researchers that 4504 doesn't like hurting people. In the second test, the Foundation really just hurt that D-Class for no reason. The thing where 4504 puts his fingers into the brain didn't require the Class-D to be conscious, that is just cruel. The last test doesn't evern require a living being at all.

-1. Sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: sorry if this is necropost, but as people have noted, their goal wasn't to maintain him happy, it was to maintain him contained, and seeing how the foundation was at that time, the way he was handled seems plausible, now from my perspective, the fact that the psys aren't helping, you and many others have said that the psys are all too stupid to be realistic except Angela, well, having a few mental health problems I have seen many psys and Angela is really the one who isn't realistic, the typical psy will look more like Weber and the others

item.title: Amazing
item.content:encoded: This has got to be my favorite SCP of all time.100% worth the read and I hope people explore this topic more often. +1 because +2 isn’t an option. (Excuse formatting, grammar, spelling, ect I’m on mobile).

item.title: Love it
item.content:encoded:
I wish I could +1 this several times. This skip really spoke to me and by the end I could feel his relief.

Very well done, thanks for contributing such great content

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It may seem like the things done to him are pretty extreme, but if you read a lot of other articles or what people say on the forums, this doesn't push too far past what a lot of people assume about the Foundation.

Great deconstruction. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Story itself is great, but believing that it took FUCKING NINE YEARS to someone coming up with idea "hey, this skip is going bananas because it want to go outside, how about we put a leash on it and take it to the walk?" is too much for me. Foundation is supposed to hire best and brightest, and these morons (sans Angela) looks like dropouts from college.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm still grateful

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just to clarify, I'm certain that I recommended this piece to you and I am not an administrator for the site. I'm operational staff, which is a step below moderator.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This by far my most favorite entry among the 4000 series. I was introduced to it by one of the administrators, to use as an example to one of my writings

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Brilliant. I didn’t get bored reading once, and I can’t say that for some articles that are 10 times shorter than this. This is a perfect example of “cold, not cruel”. You successfully made me feel bad for a murderer. And the end is heartwarming. As someone who has struggled with self-harm and isolation, the way you portrayed Dennis’ feelings was very believable to me. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: cheers

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Loved seeing a SCP's descent into madness and back.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, this is too long. The interviews in the fourth tab in particular has what is basically the same interview repeated three times: angry skip frustrated at shitty psychologist. The fact that we spend so long with the same psychologist who is horrifically bland to the point where I'm skimming his basically repetitive dialogue is also a negative.

I also find it interesting that the dude having caused the deaths of dozens of people and millions of money lost over the years due to being broken by the system didn't make the Foundation reconsider that they've been doing something wrong the whole time when Angela White proved that the skip could have been docile from the start.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for the feedback! I'm glad narrative SCPs are so well received, I might write one again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The psychiatrists are perhaps slightly more incompetent than I would like, but overall I see your point that at the end of the day they are their to contain. They aren't there for real mental health. Maybe I just imagine that they are better manipulators then.

That said, this is probably the best narrative I have read on the SCP website. I like how you take a relatively basic (by Foundation standards) SCP and give him such a strong heart. I know there is some controversy, but it really is the psych logs that make me give this a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I understand where you're coming from, but this sort of thing wasn't just "back in the 1900s". There are modern prisons today in places like America that still do things like that kind of suicide watch. There are still bad psychiatrists.

I'm not "forcing" the Foundation to do anything, and they aren't being "needlessly" cruel. I'm exploring what I see as a reasonable end result of containment. They're being "cold". Its just that in this case, and in a lot of cases, being cold and ignoring how the humanoid scp object feels and reacts to containment is a kind of cruelty.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's the thing, that was back in the early 1900's, not the present. I understand that Dr Weber doesn't personally care about Dennis, but he should be smart enough (and by extension the foundation) to know that treating someone like shit doesn't make them easier to deal with, especially when they can kill you with a touch. Waking him every half an hour isn't making it easier on anyone, just driving him closer to try and escape.

This is the same as making an article which has the foundation needlessly torture someone for fun. Yes, it will force a gut reaction of pity, but it won't be an interesting read nor will it discuss the foundation's morality. Having the foundation be stupidly cruel doesn't bring the point forward, it just drives it back through sheer ridiculousness.

Humane containment is depressing as it is and enough to write several articles out of it. Forcing the foundation to treat the anomaly like shit feels like a cheap shortcut instead of working with the more nuanced and grey-area matters.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An unreserved +1. The conduct of the psychiatrists in this piece is fucking awful, both from an ethical perspective and a practical one, but it is also entirely believable. Although I prefer interpretations of the Ethics Committee in which they are actually half-competent, this is a disturbing look at a universe in which the Foundation has dehumanized anomalous people to the point where even the Foundation forgets they used to be something other than a skip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cheers for the feedback! I've realised this one is a bit long for this website, and the things I put out in the future will probably be shorter. I'm going to work on the dialogue a lot, I've got a tale up thats all dialogue and another one in the works thats going to be characters talking at each other. I'm pleased to be getting so much feedback for my stuff, and I'm going to put it to good use, you can be sure of that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First off, let me say: I recognize Weber's behavior is not at all outside the norm. I've seen this from clinical psychiatrists firsthand. The problem with Weber isn't that he's not real. He absolutely is. Hell, I've met this fucker. No, the problem with Weber is that he doesn't "feel" real.

This is the challenge of verisimilitude: Dialogue can be accurate, but not "feel" accurate. Characters can be real, but not "feel" real. You need to present wretchedness like this with style; otherwise, most readers won't buy it. It's a hard trick to pull. Think Joffrey from Game of Thrones, or — even better because it's relevant! — Nurse Ratched from One Flew Over the Cuckoo's Nest. We buy them (and hate them) because they're stylishly horrible. You have to sell it to us.

I'm a no-vote on this. It's a very begrudging no-vote, though. I think this piece demonstrates that you've got plenty of chops; you just need a stronger sense of restraint. Some thoughts:

+ show block
– hide block
  • Most of my complaints have already been voiced by others. It's far too long (you could accomplish the same effect by cutting this down to a half, maybe even a third of its length), Dennis's characterization zig-zags (this is realistic — but again, it doesn't "feel" real), and Weber's behavior — while certainly imaginable — isn't "believable".
  • Nevertheless, the theme you're exploring here is great, and so is the way you do it. The whole story has a sort of Kafka vibe to it that I really dig… there's just way too much of it. I found myself skimming over most of the addendums except the interviews (which I found myself wanting to read — to my surprise!).
  • On that note: I think your dialogue is both… good and bad? It's hard for me to pin down: I get the sense you have a good ear for it, but you're not tightening it enough to make it really 'snap'. I enjoyed the interviews, and felt like I had a good sense of the characters, but at the same time, it often felt sloppy. Like I was reading a first draft — a good first draft, but a first draft nevertheless.
  • The fact that this piece is as long as it is — and still managed to get people to read through it and enjoy it — should probably tell you that you're doing something right.

Hopefully, none of this comes off as condescending or overbearing! I don't want to discourage you, but I also don't want to sound like a patronizing jerk. Mostly, I just think this piece has a very solid core — and as a reader, I'd definitely want to see more from you — even if this article didn't quite work for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fair enough AugSpartan, but in my defense, Dr Weber's incompetence (and the Foundation at large) is kind of the point of the piece.

Containment is locking someone up forever, usually in solitary confinement. The reason psychiatric care back in the 1900s was so shit was because it was more concerned with "managing" the mentally ill instead of helping them. Making them quieter, easier to deal with, etc. This has a lot of parallels with the Foundation, including the Foundation in the modern day.

The Foundation's goal, and by extension Dr Weber's goal, is not to help Dennis. Their goal is to keep him contained, keep him quiet, keep him obedient. When Dr Weber was going into a session with Dennis, he was doing so with an agenda. He wanted to make keeping Dennis contained easier on the Foundation, make treating Dennis easier on himself, instead of actually helping him.

I agree that Dr Weber is incompetent. But if the Foundation existed he would also be relatively normal as far as psychiatrists go.

item.title: Re: No vote
item.content:encoded:
I hear what you're saying ApollyonZ, but the cruelty/incompetence is kind of the point of the article.

What the foundation does is inherently cruel. They lock people up, usually in solitary confinement, forever. The goal of the entire organisation is to keep them there. In most mental health systems the goal is to help people, but not here. Here the goal is to "manage" the skips, keep them quiet and contained.

Thats why Weber is such a shit shrink - his goal going into his sessions with Dennis is to keep Dennis contained, not to listen to him or help him. That containment runs directly counter to Dennis' mental health, meaning that even if he wanted to, Weber simply couldn't help him.

I didn't go into it in the article, but even Dr White would have problems with him after a while. Because being "contained" is inherently bad for your mental health.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cheers, and thanks again for your critique of this piece. Without the feedback of you and the other butterflies this wouldn't exist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the response. Next time I write someone like Dr Weber I'll be sure to think about their dialogue more. People not liking Dr Weber as a character is something thats come up a few times in the responses I've gotten, but I'm having to fish that out of people not liking him because he's a deliberate example of an incompetent psychiatrist.

And I'm glad you picked up on him feeling like a different character, that was deliberate. By that point, Dennis has completely stopped giving a shit. He knows he's never escaping so he's just going to enjoy the power he gets from being able to joke about being violent towards the guards.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Cheers for picking up on those errors, I'll go in and edit them.

Thanks for the feedback on other things, but I've been working on this piece for months and I'm just happy to be done with it.

4504 is near other humanoids because he's being held at a humanoid containment site, and the frequent escapes are one of the points of the piece - if you put someone in a concrete box forever with nothing to do, they will a) spend every waking moment planning their escapes and b) hate you. One of the things I find inconsistent about other humanoid scps is that most of them do not have a history of attempting escapes. There's a reason humanoid SCP objects can't be classified as "Safe".

Weber is incompetent, but he's incompetent in a way that you can find in many clinics or prisons. He goes into his sessions with Dennis with an agenda. Instead of listening to Dennis and working on his problems, he's trying to force Dennis into a role so he can "be helped". That said, a lot of people think Weber's dialogue is flat, so if I was going to do something like this again I'd give it a few more reviews before posting.

item.title: Re: No vote
item.content:encoded:
I mean, waking someone up every half hour is definitely cruel, that is just sleep deprivation, unless the skip has been shown to not need sleep.

During my time in the mental health system, I once made a comment to one of the orderlies that the doctor I was working with was kind of rude. By the end of the week, the admin had me come to an office and document what had been said that made me think he was rude. I had to then clarify that it was more meant in jest when I said he was rude.

The point is, in an organization with any form of ethics committee, there has to be *someone* who would see this and wonder what the hell is up with that behavior.

item.title: Re: No vote
item.content:encoded: I think that's the point here, it takes that idea for what it's worth and challenges the notion by asking, "at what point does treating one coldly become cruelty?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can't for the life of me treat this seriously. Dennis' character was well done and I like the pacing of it starting from the latest file before showing us how he got there. Sadly this is all wiped away by the stupidity that is Dr. Weber. He is either stupidly incompetent, which makes the Foundation incompetent for employing him, or a straight up psychopath on a power trip, which still makes the Foundation incompetent, but this time also cruel.

I could have maybe overlooked it if this took place somewhere in the 1900's, when psychology was the equivalent of poking people in the brain and seeing what happened, but because this takes place in modern times I just can't believe the Foundation would be stupid enough to have someone like Weber be anywhere near their facilities.

The Ethic Committee stopped being a joke years ago, and it feels like the only reason Dennis is treated this badly is to force the reader to feel bad for him.

EDIT: When I call Weber "stupidity" I mean it as in calling Weber an asshole, not trying to insult the author. Realized that it didn't read as clearly as it should have.

item.title: No vote
item.content:encoded: I'm honestly torn here. On one hand, this is well written, and very immersive. On the other, the Foundation in this is just unnecessarily cruel. I lost track of how many times they pepper sprayed him. The Foundation is "cold, not cruel", but in this skip, they just come off as cruel.

item.title: Wow.
item.content:encoded: I personally will forgive smaller issues if the greater work makes up for it, and man does this do that. Dennis felt real to me, and that note at the end was terrific. This is one of, if not the best SCPs I've read in my time here. Well done!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually really like how the dates roughly correspond to the general view of How The Foundation Is reflected in the SCPs from the era

it's a plus one

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As others have said, this is really long. It took me two days to get through it, but it was a good read. It has an in-depth exploration of the psychology of a prisoner, and an indictment of the Foundation's "cold not cruel" philosophy. I was a bit skeptical of showing the current revision first, but it worked really well here: allowing me to understand why the scip behaves as it did. Also the excursion outside of the facility was just, wow.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agreed, this is the whole operational use of the Ethics Committee in the first place. He could've stayed a relatively docile Euclid if they were better at treating him like a person.

That said, we don't really know how the Ethics Committee reacted. When they reviewed the file (maybe sometime later) they may have been appalled, but you can't really reprimand a dead psychologist. Or it's par for the course. Who knows.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is exceptionally good. This is one of the best explorations of how the Foundation's recurring inhumanity can cause so much more harm than it prevents, and why the motto that the Foundation is "cold, but not cruel" is so important.

And that final Excursion Report made me feel things in my heart.

Great stuff. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The reason I mentioned Pelican Bay is to establish that the way they treated him, which you rightly call torture, is considered best practice in one of the largest facilities of its kind in the world. The Foundation psychiatric staff were being trained professionals in treating incarcerated humans, not incompetents.

The idea that sort of treatment is a monstrous abrogation of the responsibilities of mental health care and custodial detention both is only very slowly and very recently starting to circulate in anything approaching the mainstream (in the anglosphere, at least) which is part of why the progression of time in the piece is so important.

The piece is doing what a lot of good scifi tends to do and place a real issue at a slight distance to better illuminate the problems with an aspect of the world we currently live in.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The interview logs are the worst part of this article for me, but in all this was pretty disquieting and Kafkaesque (as A Random Day said). +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't think the Ethics Committee cares much about the treatment of non-anomalous prisoners in a facility they have nothing to do with. The easiest way, if possible, to keep dangerous or veil-lifting people/entities in captivity is by keeping them relatively happy. This guy basically underwent psychological torture for years at the hands of incompetent psychiatric professionals, and all the loss of staff and resources could have been averted if they'd just treated him like a person from the beginning.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Perhaps incarceration is the real horror, friend

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not particularly. The most horrifying part of this piece, as someone who used to work in CDCR, is that his treatment is basically the same as non-anomalous prisoners at Pelican Bay State Prison's 'secure housing units', where they are held in concrete cells for 22.5 hours a day and in another concrete box the rest of the time, also usually without socialization privileges.

Why would the ethics committee care about something as normal as that?

I know it's essentially a meme coming from me but this is too long, too bland, and too repetitive. I don't feel anything for Dennis, least of all pity; the only thing I feel is annoyance at the sheer incompetence of the Foundation in letting him breach so many times. If the point was to try to make the Foundation people (minus Angela) look like ice hearted monsters it falls flat because they might as well just pump him full of chemically-induced-coma juice and be done with it.

I was also hoping partway through that this would be a Builder-Bear-esque cautionary tale on letting skips talk to another, but then that didn't happen either.

Edit: also I know it's illegal to complain about my immersion breaking but goddamn does seeing "doc" in interviews take a sledgehammer to it.

item.title: Nice Job
item.content:encoded:
This gets my +1.

I love the formatting, keep up the good work.

CHEERS
-The Senpai

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Goddamn, this is a saga. It reminds me of SCP-2498 in a very good way, except instead of magic sky wizards it's a story of mental sanity, which hits much closer to home for me.

However, I did find Dr. Weber's dialogue somewhat dense and flat here, as mentioned above. But, I'll be damned if I never read a humanoid anomaly in the same way again.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I… wow.

There's some writing gaffes which others have ably pointed out, and Weber is a tad dense, but overall this is a really powerful piece on the potential horror in anomalous human containment. Big +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-4504: “Angela White”, huh? Your parents musta been so proud of that one.

I'm deeply ashamed to admit that I understood this reference.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Personally, I think the current documentation being at the front like it now is very effective for the article. It sets up the thing to be a scary monster and then works its magic when it starts to pick up after the second tab.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seems like something the Ethics Committee would freak out over if they found out how badly this poor sap's been treated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Personally I read it as the Australian branch of the Foundation and whatever that site was (that apparently isn't Site-17) being horribly incompetent1 and Angela being one of the only people at the facility that had any idea how the hell to do things. Like maybe the people at the site are more used to friendly and "wholesome" SCPs and have no idea what to do when one is just pissed and wants out.

Footnotes
1. But still incompetent is a believable way, mind you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i'm willing to forgive a lot more dialogue fallacies than most people (particularly in this comment section) so i personally found this to be some very fucked up shit. in the middle i was genuinely scared for the fate of this character.

some suggestions:

put the current documentation last

as the current foundation has evolved to be more caring, maybe change the dates to the early 2000s or something? combined with the crosslink, this seems and reads like an extended and really good 2008/9 SCP.

+1 since I want to see this survive, but some changes could push this from good to great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's one thing for the tests and oversecurity to be done, but man, that Site must be pretty terrible if there are other people like Dr. Weber working there. His dialog seems cold in a flatly-written way, rather than in a realistic way. Normally I wouldn't examine that aspect too much, but he's a central character here, and although there's clearly an angle being built there with him, I also can't help but wonder if the article's also painting him as just a regular dude that happened to totally break a man due to how he comes off from the dialog.

I like it, on the assumption that you were writing to tell us a story about particularly cruel and uncaring Foundation researchers breaking an anomaly because they see him as one (which is how I read it and like to believe you wrote it). EDIT: A thing I noticed as it went on was that 4504 shifted in personality to cracking jokes and sarcasm, and I don't mind this inherently, but I dunno. It felt off here, almost like a completely different character from another article.

I still like the core concept and where you take this at a number of points, but I think the flat characterization/dialog for me on the whole overshadows what I like about it, enough to downvote. But good job going for this all the same! Longform pieces with a sense of clarity and direction are always cool as heck and you went for a concept I haven't seen explored much on the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wowzers. I didn't think I would finish this in one sitting, but here I am an hour and a half later.

This is an amazing piece. Not only does it serve as an examination of character for Dennis, but it also exemplifies the Foundation's treatment of such anomalies. Somehow, in the middle of it, I got to thinking about the years used in the documentation. There was this thought that this wasn't just a timeline of Dennis's health, but kind of also how the writers of the site evolved and changed in the way they wrote their anomalies and containment of them. It might not have been your intention, author, but it was something that I thought about.

All-in-all, I loved this. Excellent work. (though you may want to take a moment to proofread again, just in case)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dr Weber: Now really, Dennis. You cannot keep things like this to yourself. You must talk to me about them, it’s what I’m here for.

SCP-4504: No, Doc, please- huhh, don’t interrupt me. I’m- It’s hard to concentrate.

Dr Weber: What the devil is the matter with you?

Pushed me out of the immersion given Weber changes from 0 to 1 when the plot wants him to.

Reread it two times so I could be sure it wasn't my eyes or something but I don't really get why Weber states "what the devil is the matter with you." If its to convey impatience or lording over the patient, I don't really get that from the overall character of Weber as he's somewhat of a blank slate filled with the goals of the Foundation itself. To protect coldly.

Maybe if I believed Weber wasn't just a foil for Dennis and had his own ambitions leading up to the tone shift, I would upvote it. As it stands, it's a solid piece but doesn't sit well with me in terms of narrative inconsistency.

The Pros of the piece.
>>>

EDIT: I think its something very small that only bugs me, and I do hope this does well with others. It has a solid progression, the confessions of Dennis only make his situation worse, its very clear that the Foundation's protocols are noncompatible with the humanoid's condition and have only exaggerated his mental illness rather than calm him down to a restful or amicable state.

EDIT-EDIT:

Minor SpaGR issues I detected

Haven’t had a suicidal though all week

thought*

After being returning to its containment cell prematurely

returned*

And another huge logic issue I picked up on the third reading. Why is the Foundation putting this anomaly near other humanoids?

freeing two dozen other humanoid SCP objects. Among those that breached containment were SCP-4504 and SCP-082.

This seems like it happens far too often then it should. Like, the way it escapes and the way it attempts suicide are fine and fun because you can see the cogworks in Dennis' head as he responds to every situation. But the fact he's constantly near other humanoids? It makes the Foundation seem extremely inept and piles onto the weird inconsistencies from beforehand with Weber's impatience.

TL;DR: I want to reiterate, I reread this four times because I loved the direction and premise. Truly, I really do want to upvote this on wikidot after a few changes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a brutally long piece, but its Kafkaesque depiction of Dennis Huang's deterioration genuinely made me believe that he could snap the way he did. Two major notes of criticism: Dennis's use of 'Doc' in the second tab made me roll my eyes — when was the last time you ever called someone Doc — and in-universe, blackboxing the name of the medications that are used to treat SCP-4504 in the containment procedures is bafflingly idiotic. How is the Foundation supposed to treat Dennis if nobody knows what he's supposed to be treated with?
